The Liberal Democrats have an ideology that draws on both the liberal and social democratic traditions.[[183]](#cite_note-SeydWhiteley2006-183) The party is primarily [social liberal](/wiki/Social_liberal "Social liberal"), supporting redistribution but sceptical of increasing the power of the state, emphasising the link between equality and liberty. The party supports investment and progressive taxation, but also promotes [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") and a less centralised economy.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-184) This distinguishes the party from many liberal parties elsewhere in Europe that are instead dominated by [classical liberalism](/wiki/Classical_liberalism "Classical liberalism").[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-:1-186) By comparison, the Liberal Democrats support a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") and have sometimes opposed privatisation.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-184)

The party spans the [centre](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") and [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left "Centre-left"), sometimes considered [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing "Left-wing") due to its stance on [social progressivism](/wiki/Social_progressivism "Social progressivism") and opposition to [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing") parties and opinions, and has emphasised each aspect at different times.[[186]](#cite_note-:1-186)[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeadowcroft2000-187)[[188]](#cite_note-188)[[189]](#cite_note-:2-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190) The public have traditionally viewed the party as centre-left,[[191]](#cite_note-191) though during the [Cameron–Clegg coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") they were seen as centrist.[[192]](#cite_note-192) On economic issues, the party has usually been positioned between the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") and the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), though typically closer to the Labour Party.[[193]](#cite_note-:3-193) There is a degree of ideological diversity among members of the Liberal Democrats, with a wide range of opinions on most subjects.[[183]](#cite_note-SeydWhiteley2006-183)

[Leonard Hobhouse](/wiki/Leonard_Hobhouse "Leonard Hobhouse") is a key ideological influence on the Liberal Democrats, and there is substantial overlap between the party's platform and the form of social democracy advocated by [Anthony Crosland](/wiki/Anthony_Crosland "Anthony Crosland") in *[The Future of Socialism](/wiki/The_Future_of_Socialism "The Future of Socialism")*.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-184)[[194]](#cite_note-Evans20132-194) The party's [egalitarianism](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") is based on the concept of [equality of opportunity](/wiki/Equality_of_opportunity "Equality of opportunity"). The party has been sceptical of [positive discrimination](/wiki/Positive_discrimination "Positive discrimination"), including in its process for selecting political candidates. The party has frequently debated the introduction of [all-women shortlists](/wiki/All-women_shortlist "All-women shortlist") in selection, but not implemented them.[[194]](#cite_note-Evans20132-194)

The Liberal Democrats support a range of constitutional reforms, including by advocating a [decentralised federal structure for the United Kingdom](/wiki/Federalism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Federalism in the United Kingdom"), including devolving power to the [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England").[[195]](#cite_note-195) The party supported devolution to Scotland and Wales enacted by the Labour government under [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair"). The party has consistently supported [electoral reform](/wiki/Electoral_reform "Electoral reform") to produce more proportional results.[[196]](#cite_note-196) On social issues, the party is liberal and progressive. It has consistently supported LGBT rights and drug reform.[[19]](#cite_note-HauteClose20192-19) The party is internationalist and pro-European. They have consistently supported policies of European integration, including long-term advocacy of the [United Kingdom adopting the euro](/wiki/United_Kingdom_and_the_euro "United Kingdom and the euro"),[[197]](#cite_note-197) though they have opposed the establishing of a [European army](/wiki/European_army "European army").[[186]](#cite_note-:1-186)[[198]](#cite_note-RussellRussell2005-198) Both before and after the [2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"), the party has advocated for the United Kingdom's continued membership of the European Union. The party supports [liberal interventionism](/wiki/Liberal_interventionism "Liberal interventionism"), and supported the [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), later opposing the [2003 invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq") due to its lack of support from the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations").[[193]](#cite_note-:3-193) The party has also faced internal division over the issue of [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear weapons of the United Kingdom").[[189]](#cite_note-:2-189)

The party has a number of factions representing different strains of liberal thought.[[19]](#cite_note-HauteClose20192-19)[[199]](#cite_note-:5-199) Although the social liberals, represented by the [Social Liberal Forum](/wiki/Social_Liberal_Forum "Social Liberal Forum") (often abbreviated to the SLF), are the majority, factions that advocate for more [economically liberal](/wiki/Economically_liberal "Economically liberal") positions include [Liberal Reform](/wiki/Liberal_Reform "Liberal Reform") (often abbreviated to LR) and the "Orange Bookers", named after *[The Orange Book: Reclaiming Liberalism](/wiki/The_Orange_Book:_Reclaiming_Liberalism "The Orange Book: Reclaiming Liberalism")*; *The Orange Book* is most often associated with former deputy prime minister [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg"), who contributed to it, along with former Liberal Democrat leader [Vince Cable](/wiki/Vince_Cable "Vince Cable") and incumbent leader [Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey").[[199]](#cite_note-:5-199)[[200]](#cite_note-:4-200) Additionally, there is the centre-left [Beveridge Group](/wiki/Beveridge_Group "Beveridge Group"), inspired by [William Beveridge](/wiki/William_Beveridge "William Beveridge"). The Beveridge Group has been associated with both social liberals and social democrats within the party, including former Liberal Democrat leader [Charles Kennedy](/wiki/Charles_Kennedy "Charles Kennedy").[[201]](#cite_note-201)

## Policy platform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Policy platform")]

### Constitutional reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Constitutional reform")]

See also: [Constitutional reform in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitutional_reform_in_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional reform in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Liberal_Democrat_Conference_2011.jpg/330px-Liberal_Democrat_Conference_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberal_Democrat_Conference_2011.jpg)

The 2011 Liberal Democrats conference

The Liberal Democrats support institutional reform in the United Kingdom, including the decentralisation of state power, reform of Parliament, and electoral reform.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200626-202) At its 1993 conference, the party put forward plans for the introduction of [fixed term parliaments](/wiki/Fixed_term_parliament "Fixed term parliament"),[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010218-203) something it later secured in the coalition government of 2010 to 2015.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010321-107) Also in 1993, it proposed state funding for political parties.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010218-203) The Liberal Democrats have long included a commitment to proportional representation in their manifestos.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010256-204) According to the *[New Statesman](/wiki/New_Statesman "New Statesman")*, this is the "one policy with which the Liberal Democrats are identified in the minds of the public."[[205]](#cite_note-205) The Lib Dems calls for devolution or [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") for Scotland and Wales were enacted by Blair's Labour government in the late 1990s.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010239-63) The 1993 conference also called for the introduction of a [bill of rights](/wiki/Bill_of_rights "Bill of rights") into the British constitution.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010218-203) Its 2001 manifesto included a commitment to lowering the [voting age](/wiki/Voting_age "Voting age") from 18 to 16.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010256-204) In 2013, an internal pressure group in the party called *Liberal Democrats for a republic* was formed.[[206]](#cite_note-206)

According to a 1999 survey, two-thirds of party members supported retaining the monarchy.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200626–27-207) In the 1990s, there was an anti-royalist contingent within the party;[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010225-208) in 1993, the party conference announced support for removing the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative_in_the_United_Kingdom "Royal prerogative in the United Kingdom"),[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010218-203) and the 2000 conference backed calls for the monarch to be removed as the [Supreme Governor](/wiki/Supreme_Governor "Supreme Governor") of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010255-209) At its 2003 conference, the party's Youth and Student League put forward a motion calling for the abolition of the monarchy and the introduction of an elected [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010267-75) The 2000 party conference produced a call for the [1701 Act of Settlement](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701") to be reformed so as to allow the heir to the throne to marry a Roman Catholic,[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010255-209) while the party's 2001 manifesto called for the [disestablishment](/wiki/Disestablishmentarianism "Disestablishmentarianism") of the Church of England.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010256-204) The party's endorsement of [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism") dates back to 1990, with standing policy favouring total [separation of church and state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state").[[210]](#cite_note-210)

### Economic and social welfare policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economic and social welfare policy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/LibDem_posterboards_Hornsey_Wood_Green_7_May_2015.jpg/250px-LibDem_posterboards_Hornsey_Wood_Green_7_May_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:LibDem_posterboards_Hornsey_Wood_Green_7_May_2015.jpg)

Liberal Democrats campaigning stakeboards in [Hornsey and Wood Green](/wiki/Hornsey_and_Wood_Green "Hornsey and Wood Green") in 2015

The 1999 membership survey found that most favoured free markets and individual responsibility; they were nevertheless split on whether or not they regarded private enterprise as the best way to solve economic problems.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200628-211) Most were against either further privatisation or further nationalisation, although they were overwhelmingly favourable to increasing taxation and government spending.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200629-212) The membership was also heavily against additional restrictions on trade unions.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200629-212)

Liberal Democrat policy has generally been favourable to social welfare spending.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200686-213) During the 2000s, the party made pledges for major investment into health, education, and public services.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010256-204) In 1995, the party announced a plan to put £2 billion into education, including nursery places for under fives,[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010228-214) while its 2005 manifesto included a commitment to use £1.5 billion to decrease class sizes in schools.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010275-84) In the 2000s, the party also pledged to abolish tuition fees for university students,[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010268-215) and in the build-up to the 2010 general election, Clegg pledged that under a Lib Dem government this would be achieved over six years.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010307-216) In 2004, it pledged to add £25 a week to the [state pension](/wiki/State_pension "State pension") for people over the age of 75.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010273-217) In 2003, it outlined plans for devolving control of schools to local councils.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010268-215)

In the mid-1990s and early 2000s, it stated that such increases in education spending would be funded through higher taxes. These included a 50% tax on those earning over £100,000 a year,[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010228,_256-218) and raising the [basic rate](/wiki/Income_tax_in_the_United_Kingdom "Income tax in the United Kingdom") of income tax by one penny in the pound.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010233,_256-219) In 2003, the party's conference approved plans for a local income tax of 3.5 pence in the pound that would replace [council tax](/wiki/Council_tax "Council tax"); the party believed that this would result in 70% of the population paying less tax.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010267-75) In 2006, the party abandoned its plans for a 50% tax on the highest earners,[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010288-220) and also put forward plans to cut income tax but balance the books by increasing tax on air travel and introducing a carbon tax.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010288-220)

Under Clegg, the party emphasised lowering taxes rather than raising them; it stated that a 4 pence reduction in the basic rate tax could be permitted by finding £20 billion savings in Whitehall. This measure was opposed by the left of the party.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010298-98) Amid the 2008 recession, Clegg called for £20 billion cuts to state spending, to be funded by measures like reducing the number of people eligible for [tax credits](/wiki/Tax_credit "Tax credit") and scrapping road building schemes.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010299-221) In its 2010 manifesto, it pledged to end income taxes for those earning under £10,000 a year,[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010309-222) something it introduced through the Cameron coalition government.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010321-107) Also in 2010, it stated that it would halve the national deficit over the course of four years.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010307-216) It had also specified that it would oppose any increases in VAT, although when in coalition announced an increase in VAT to 20%.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010324-108)

### Foreign policy and the European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Foreign policy and the European Union")]

The Liberal Democrats supported the [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") in 2001.[[223]](#cite_note-223) The party was the only one of Britain's three major parties to oppose the [2003 invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq").[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006146–147-224) The party's leadership stressed that this was not because the party was intrinsically anti-war, but because the invasion did not have support from the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations").[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrayson20076-225) In the wake of the invasion, the party's 2005 manifesto included a pledge that the UK would never again support a military occupation deemed illegal under international law.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010275-84) [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell") demanded the suspension of all future arms exports to [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") during the [2006 Lebanon War](/wiki/2006_Lebanon_War "2006 Lebanon War") and [Operation Summer Rains](/wiki/Operation_Summer_Rains "Operation Summer Rains").[[226]](#cite_note-226) [Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey") and the Liberal Democrats have supported a ceasefire in the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") since 13 November 2023.[[227]](#cite_note-227)

The Liberal Democrats called for a full [judicial inquiry](/wiki/Judicial_inquiry "Judicial inquiry") into Britain's involvement in [CIA](/wiki/CIA "CIA") [black sites](/wiki/Black_site "Black site") and [extraordinary rendition](/wiki/Extraordinary_rendition "Extraordinary rendition") since the 11 [September attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[228]](#cite_note-228) They also called on the UK government to suspend [arms sales](/wiki/UK_arms_export "UK arms export") to [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and condemned the [Saudi-led coalition](/wiki/Saudi-led_intervention_in_the_Yemeni_civil_war "Saudi-led intervention in the Yemeni civil war")'s attacks [targeting civilians in Yemen](/wiki/War_crimes_in_the_Yemeni_civil_war_(2014%E2%80%93present) "War crimes in the Yemeni civil war (2014–present)").[[229]](#cite_note-229)[[230]](#cite_note-230) In February 2019, the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") issued [an advisory opinion stating](/wiki/Legal_Consequences_of_the_Separation_of_the_Chagos_Archipelago_from_Mauritius_in_1965 "Legal Consequences of the Separation of the Chagos Archipelago from Mauritius in 1965") that the UK must transfer the [Chagos Archipelago](/wiki/Chagos_Archipelago "Chagos Archipelago") to [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") as they were [not legally separated](/wiki/Chagos_Archipelago_sovereignty_dispute "Chagos Archipelago sovereignty dispute") from the latter in 1965.[[231]](#cite_note-231) Liberal Democrat foreign affairs spokesperson [Alistair Carmichael](/wiki/Alistair_Carmichael "Alistair Carmichael") stated: "The ICJ has very clearly instructed the UK to return the island chain to Mauritian control. The government's refusal to do so is arrogant and jeopardises our credibility on a world stage."[[232]](#cite_note-232)

Whiteley et al. noted that "like the Liberals before them, [the Liberal Democrats] have taken a strong positive position on internationalism", including the need for international cooperation, aid for the developing world, and European integration.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200612-233) In this they have always been more internationalist and pro-Europeanist than either Labour or the Conservatives.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200612-233)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Liberal_Democrats_%22Bollocks_to_Brexit%22_campaigning_2019.jpg/250px-Liberal_Democrats_%22Bollocks_to_Brexit%22_campaigning_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberal_Democrats_%22Bollocks_to_Brexit%22_campaigning_2019.jpg)

Following the 2016 referendum which produced a majority in favour of Brexit, the Lib Dems campaigned against the decision with its somewhat controversial "Bollocks to Brexit" campaign

From its foundation, the Liberal Democrats were committed to the UK's membership of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010205-48) In 1993, it called for the UK to take a lead in seeking a timetable for the adoption of a pan-European currency, and also called for the formation of an autonomous European central bank.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010218-203) A 1999 survey of party members found they overwhelmingly backed European integration, and two thirds wanted the UK to adopt the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") currency.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200627-234) In its 1999 manifesto for the European Parliamentary elections, it called for completing the European single market, holding a referendum on the adoption of the euro currency, establishing an EU constitution, expanding the EU into Central and Eastern Europe, and encouraging an EU-wide clampdown on pollution and international crime.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010247-235) This attitude had been inherited from the Liberal Party which had originally proposing membership into the predecessor [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community").[[236]](#cite_note-Jones2007-236) However, the Liberal Democrats oppose the [European federalism](/wiki/Federalisation_of_the_European_Union "Federalisation of the European Union") espoused by their counterparts.[[237]](#cite_note-Geddes2013-237)

Despite its pro-European stance, the party has included [Eurosceptics](/wiki/Eurosceptics "Eurosceptics") such as the MP [Nick Harvey](/wiki/Nick_Harvey "Nick Harvey").[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010249-238) The 1999 membership survey found that 37% wanted the UK to remain in the EU but to have the latter's powers reduced while 5% of members wanted the UK to leave the EU altogether.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200628-211) Cook argued that whereas the Lib Dems were once "the most pro-European of all British parties", by 2008, it had "a vocal Eurosceptic element" who were opposed to the British ratification of the EU's [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Lisbon_Treaty "Lisbon Treaty") without a referendum.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010294-239) Under Clegg, the party backed away from its hardline pro-EU position.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010298-98)

In June 2016, following the [United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") in which 51.9% voted in favour of leaving the European Union, [Tim Farron](/wiki/Tim_Farron "Tim Farron") said that if Liberal Democrats were to be elected in the next parliamentary election, they would not follow through with triggering [Article 50](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Consolidated_version_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union/Title_VI:_Final_Provisions#Article_50 "s:Consolidated version of the Treaty on European Union/Title VI: Final Provisions") of the [Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_European_Union "Treaty on European Union") and [leaving the EU](/wiki/Leaving_the_EU "Leaving the EU") ("Any Member State may decide to withdraw from the Union in accordance with its own constitutional requirements") but would instead keep UK part of the EU.[[240]](#cite_note-240) Following this promise, the Liberal Democrats claim that their membership has increased by 10,000 since the referendum; at one point, the growth in the party was the equivalent of one person joining per minute.[[241]](#cite_note-241) Campaigning for a second referendum regarding the exact goals of Brexit negotiation was one of the party's flagship policies in the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election") and the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election").[[242]](#cite_note-242)

### Environmentalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Environmentalism")]

The Liberal Democrats have strongly advocated for environmental protection and have typically taken more radical stances on environmental issues than either Labour or the Conservatives. One of the Party's Affiliated Organisations, the Green Liberal Democrats (GLD), lays claim to being the "Environmental Conscience" of the party and was formed in 1988 at the same time as the Liberal Party merged with the SDP to become the Liberal Democrats. (See article on [Green liberalism](/wiki/Green_liberalism "Green liberalism"))
GLD itself was a merger between the small SDP Green group and the Liberal Ecology Group (LEG) which was formed in 1977.
Amongst the Class of 2024 Lib Dem MPs, 26 of them are paid-up members of GLD (including Party Leader, Sir Ed Davey), thus forming a significant green caucus in the new parliament.
[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200613-243) In 1993, the party put forward proposals for an EU tax on energy use and CO2 emissions.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010218-203) That year, it also proposed that GDP should be redefined to take into account pollution and the depletion of natural resources.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010219-58) At its 2009 conference, the party introduced a commitment for Lib Dem controlled councils to cut their carbon emissions by 10% in 2010.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010306-244) Other policies included:

* Designate an ecologically coherent network of marine protected areas with appropriate management by 2020.[[245]](#cite_note-Manifesto-245)
* Encourage the uptake of water metering, including introducing metering in all defined water-stressed areas by 2025, coupled with the development of national social tariffs to protect low income households.[[245]](#cite_note-Manifesto-245)
* Complete the coastal path, introduce a fuller Right to Roam and a new designation of National Nature Parks to protect up to a million acres of accessible green space valued by local communities.[[245]](#cite_note-Manifesto-245)

The manifesto for the 2024 General Election contained significant references to the need to address Climate Change urgently and put the issue of Restoration of Nature much higher up the political agenda. Amongst the issues that caught the serious attention of the electorate was the impact of sewage spills into the environment and the need to tackle the inrresponsibility of many of the water companies.

### Human rights and individual liberty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Human rights and individual liberty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Lib_Dem_Trafalgar_Square_Flashmob_%284582025906%29.jpg/250px-Lib_Dem_Trafalgar_Square_Flashmob_%284582025906%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lib_Dem_Trafalgar_Square_Flashmob_(4582025906).jpg)

Members of a Lib Dems [flash mob](/wiki/Flash_mob "Flash mob") in London's [Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square") in the build-up to the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election")

The Liberal Democrats place greater emphasis on [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") and individual freedoms than the Conservatives or Labour.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200614-246) Conversely, the political scientist John Meadowcroft expressed the view that "the Liberal Democrats are a supposedly liberal party that does not believe in liberty."[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeadowcroft200893-247) Commenting on the 1999 membership survey, Whiteley et al. noted that the majority of members took "a distinctly right of centre view" on many, although not all, moral and legal issues.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200632-248)

Its 1997 manifesto committed the party to lowering the [age of consent](/wiki/Age_of_consent "Age of consent") for same-sex couples to 16, bringing it in line with that of opposite-sex couples.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010233-61) At its 2000 conference, party delegates backed calls for the government to provide legal recognition for same-sex relationships.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010255-209) In the 1999 membership survey, 57% believed that the government should discourage the growth of one-parent families.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200632-248) That same survey found just over half of the party membership expressing pro-choice views regarding abortion access.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200631-249)

At its 1997 conference, the party's delegates voted in favour of establishing a Royal Commission to examine the possibility of decriminalising [voluntary euthanasia](/wiki/Voluntary_euthanasia "Voluntary euthanasia").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010240-65)

At its 1994 conference, party delegates voted to end criminal prosecutions for cannabis possession, although the party's 23 MPs voted against the measure.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010225-208) The 1999 membership survey suggested a tougher stance on many law and order issues, with over half wanting longer sentencing and no option of parole for those serving life sentences.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200632-248) The 2004 party congress approved a ban on smoking in public places.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010269-250)

In March 2016, the Liberal Democrats became the first major political party in the UK to support the legalisation of cannabis. The party supports cannabis sale and possession to be legal for all UK adults aged 18-years-old and over, the set up of specialist licensed stores to sell cannabis, the legalisation of home cultivation of cannabis for personal use, small scale cannabis clubs to be licensed, and a new regulator to oversee the market.[[251]](#cite_note-251)[[252]](#cite_note-252)

## Organisation and structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Organisation and structure")]

See also: [Federal Board (Liberal Democrats)](/wiki/Federal_Board_(Liberal_Democrats) "Federal Board (Liberal Democrats)"), [English Liberal Democrats](/wiki/English_Liberal_Democrats "English Liberal Democrats"), [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats"), and [Welsh Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Welsh_Liberal_Democrats "Welsh Liberal Democrats")

The Liberal Democrats are a [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") party of the parties of England, Scotland, and Wales. The English and Scottish parties are further split into regions. The parliamentary parties of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") and the [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") form semi-autonomous units within the party. The leaders in the House of Commons and the Scottish Parliament are the leaders of the federal party and the Scottish Party; the leaders in the other two chambers, and the officers of all parliamentary parties, are elected from their own number. Co-ordination of all party activities across all federated groups is undertaken through the Federal Board. Chaired by the party president, its 30+ members includes representatives from each of the groups and democratically elected representatives.[[253]](#cite_note-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/ScottishLiberalDemocratsNoEntry-Stornoway-Scotland-20100407.jpg/250px-ScottishLiberalDemocratsNoEntry-Stornoway-Scotland-20100407.jpg)](/wiki/File:ScottishLiberalDemocratsNoEntry-Stornoway-Scotland-20100407.jpg)

Campaign board for the Scottish Liberal Democrats in [Stornoway](/wiki/Stornoway "Stornoway")

In the first quarter of 2008, the party received £1.1 million in donations and had total borrowings and unused credit facilities of £1.1 million (the "total debt" figure reported by the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)") includes, for example, unused overdraft facilities). This compared to Labour's £3.1 million in donations and £17.8 million of borrowing/credit facilities and the Conservative's £5.7 million in donations and £12.1 million of borrowing/credit facilities.[[254]](#cite_note-2008finances-254)

[Specified Associated Organisations](/wiki/Specified_Associated_Organisation "Specified Associated Organisation") (SAOs) review and input policies, representing groups including: [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities "Ethnic minorities") (LDCRE),[[255]](#cite_note-255) women (WLD),[[256]](#cite_note-256) the LGBT community ([LGBT+ Liberal Democrats](/wiki/LGBT%2B_Liberal_Democrats "LGBT+ Liberal Democrats")),[[257]](#cite_note-257) youth and students ([Young Liberals](/wiki/Young_Liberals_(United_Kingdom) "Young Liberals (United Kingdom)")), engineers and scientists (ALDES),[[258]](#cite_note-258) parliamentary candidates (PCA)[[259]](#cite_note-259) and local [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor") (ALDC).[[260]](#cite_note-260) Others can become Associated Organisations (AOs) as campaigning or representative groups in the party, such as the Green Liberal Democrats (GLD),[[261]](#cite_note-261) the Liberal Democrat European Group (LDEG)[[262]](#cite_note-262) and the Liberal Democrat Disability Association.[[263]](#cite_note-263) There are many other groups that are not formally affiliated to the party, including Social Liberal Forum (SLF)[[264]](#cite_note-264) and Liberal Reform.[[265]](#cite_note-265)

Like the Conservatives, the Lib Dems organise in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"). Although they do not contest elections in the province, they work with the [Alliance Party of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland"), described as its sister party[[266]](#cite_note-266) and *de facto* agreeing to support the Alliance in elections.[[267]](#cite_note-allianceNI-267) There is a separate local party operating in Northern Ireland, the [Northern Ireland Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Liberal_Democrats "Northern Ireland Liberal Democrats").[[268]](#cite_note-268) It is also a sister party of the [Liberal Party of Gibraltar](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Gibraltar "Liberal Party of Gibraltar") and contested the South-West England constituency at European Parliamentary elections on a [joint ticket](/wiki/Joint_ticket "Joint ticket"); they used to take place six on the [party list](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation").[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270)

The party is a member of [Liberal International](/wiki/Liberal_International "Liberal International") and the [Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe Party](/wiki/Alliance_of_Liberals_and_Democrats_for_Europe_Party "Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe Party"). Their 16 MEPs sat in the [Renew Europe](/wiki/Renew_Europe "Renew Europe") group in the European Parliament until [Britain left the European Union.](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit")[[271]](#cite_note-271) The party colour is [amber](/wiki/Amber_(color) "Amber (color)"), but it is referred to as [yellow](/wiki/Yellow "Yellow") in the party's style guide.[[272]](#cite_note-272) The party anthem is the old [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)")'s "[The Land](/wiki/The_Land_(song) "The Land (song)")" while its slogan is "Build a Brighter Future".[[273]](#cite_note-273) The party headquarters are at 8–10 Great George Street [London](/wiki/London "London") SW1P 3AE.[[274]](#cite_note-274)

## Support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Support")]

In the 2005 general election, the party was endorsed by *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")*.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010275-84) Cook noted that in the build-up to the 2010 election, most mainstream press—which was aligned with either the Conservatives or Labour—was "voraciously hostile" to the Lib Dems.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010316-275) In that election, it nevertheless attracted the endorsement of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* and *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer "The Observer")*.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010310-276)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Finances")]

Whereas Labour gained funding through its links to trade unions and the Conservatives through big business, the Liberal Democrats have relied on funds raised by the subscriptions and donations provided by its members.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006173-277) The party had some major donors, such as Lord Jacobs, who gave it around £1 million over the course of twenty years until he resigned in 2008.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010298-98) In some years, it struggled to cover its costs; for instance, it made a loss of £670,000 in 2008.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010304-278)

### Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Membership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Birmingham%27s_Bin-Brexit_rally_for_the_Conservative_Party_conference%2C_September_30%2C_2018_25.jpg/250px-Birmingham%27s_Bin-Brexit_rally_for_the_Conservative_Party_conference%2C_September_30%2C_2018_25.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birmingham%27s_Bin-Brexit_rally_for_the_Conservative_Party_conference,_September_30,_2018_25.jpg)

The Liberal Democrat contingent at an anti-Brexit rally in Birmingham in September 2018

In its early years, the caricature of Liberal Democrat members was that of "sandal-wearing, bearded eccentrics obsessed by the minutiae of electoral reform".[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010261-73) Based on their 1999 survey of Liberal Democrat members, Whiteley noted that although party members shared many of the same attitudes as the party's voters, there were also "striking differences", namely in that members were "older, more middle-class and better educated" than the voters.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200617-279) Their survey found that party membership was 54% male;[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200622–23-280) and was dominated by middle-class people, with working-class individuals comprising only 5% of members (in contrast to 30% of Labour and 19% of Conservative members at that time).[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200624–25-281) The average age was 59, and 58% of members were aged 56 or over.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200623-282) A third were retired, and a third in full-time employment.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200624-283) A majority worked, or had previously worked, in the non-profit sector.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200624-283) 42% possessed a degree, which was higher than among Labour (30%) and Conservative (19%) members at that time.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200623-282) 65% of members considered themselves religious, with 70% of those being [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), 15% [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), and 11% [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic").[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200625-284)

As of 1999, 43% of members had previously belonged to the Liberal Party, 14% to the Social Democratic Party, and 42% had joined with no previous political affiliation.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200655-285) 21% of members had joined because of their social contacts, such as friends, family, and colleagues, who were already members.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200668–69-286) Around 40% of members stated that they joined because they agreed with the party's principles; a further 16% said they joined because of its policies.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200669-287) The majority of members were largely inactive in party activities, with only 22% of those polled indicating that they were willing to attend party meetings.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200671–72-288)

The senior ranks of the party had long been heavily male-dominated; after the 1997 general election, for instance, only three of its 46 MPs were women.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200622-289) Reinforcing its "male, middle-class image", after the 2010 election, 40% of Liberal Democrat MPs were privately educated.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010312-290) However, following the 2019 general election, seven of its then eleven MPs were female, and the Lib Dem victories in the [2021 Chesham and Amersham by-election](/wiki/2021_Chesham_and_Amersham_by-election "2021 Chesham and Amersham by-election"), followed by the [2021 North Shropshire by-election](/wiki/2021_North_Shropshire_by-election "2021 North Shropshire by-election"), increased the share to nine out of thirteen MPs.

Membership fluctuated between 1988 and 2000 between a low of 69,000 in 2000 and a peak of 101,768 in 1994.[[291]](#cite_note-memstats-291) Membership increased sharply after the confirmation on 18 April 2017 of the [8 June 2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), surpassing 100,000 on 24 April 2017[[292]](#cite_note-292) and reached an all-time high in June 2019 following the [2019 European elections](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2019 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"),[[293]](#cite_note-Liberal_Democrats/Greg_Foster-293) increasing further after their win in the [Brecon and Radnorshire by-election](/wiki/2019_Brecon_and_Radnorshire_by-election "2019 Brecon and Radnorshire by-election") which reduced the working majority of the [Conservative government](/wiki/First_Johnson_ministry "First Johnson ministry") to just one seat.[[294]](#cite_note-auto-294)

In 2019, the party had a minimum of 17,102 registered supporters which were not included in the membership figure of at least 120,000 members.[[294]](#cite_note-auto-294)

A research briefing paper by the [House of Commons Library](/wiki/House_of_Commons_Library "House of Commons Library") published on 30 August 2022, stated that data submitted to the Electoral Commission suggested that the party membership at the end of 2021 stood at 73,544.[[295]](#cite_note-295)

According to the December 2024 party accounts, the party membership dropped to 60,000. With registered supporters, the party had 83,174 members/supporters.[[2]](#cite_note-BBC_2025_membership-2)

### Voters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Voters")]

The 1997 British Election Study Survey found that the average Liberal Democrat voter was aged 47, with 52% between the ages of 18 and 45.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200636–37-297) At the time, 16% of Lib Dem voters possessed a degree.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200636–37-297) Working class or blue collar workers composed 23% of Lib Dem voters, a much higher percentage than was found among the party's membership.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200636–37-297) The survey found that Liberal Democrat voters shared many attitudes with the members; these voters overwhelmingly desired proportional representation and 63% backed EU membership.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200637–38-298) Where the voters differed from the members was on the issue of foreign aid; over half of members wanted to increase the UK's foreign aid budget, whereas only a third of Liberal Democrat voters agreed.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200641–42-299)

Analysing voting patterns from the 1990s, Whiteley et al. argued that highly educated people were more likely than average to vote Liberal Democrat, that older people were less likely than average to vote Liberal Democrat, and that class, gender, or ethnicity had no impact on the tendency to vote for the party.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006163-300)

[Ipsos](/wiki/Ipsos "Ipsos") studied voter patterns for the 2010 and 2015 elections. Their support in 2010 came from a fairly even spread of ages; at 5% to 10% of all the age groups studied, peaking in the 35 to 44 range. At the 2015 election, their vote across all age groups declined, but most strongly among younger voters.[[301]](#cite_note-301)

From the Liberal Party, the Liberal Democrats inherited a strong base in Wales and Scotland.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst20066-302) In 2010, Cook noted that the party's safe seats "do not fit a very homogeneous pattern", being scattered amidst rural, middle-class suburban, and inner city areas.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010313–314-303) A key feature of the party's electoral strategy has been foregrounding community politics.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst200689-304) Examining the survey evidence, Whiteley et al. argued that the strength of grassroots party activism in a particular area had a big impact on the vote share that the Liberal Democrats received there.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006135-305)

### General elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: General elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Historic_Lib_Dem_vote_share_and_seat_share_1983-2024.svg/500px-Historic_Lib_Dem_vote_share_and_seat_share_1983-2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Historic_Lib_Dem_vote_share_and_seat_share_1983-2024.svg)

Liberal Democrats vote and seat share, 1983–2024

Throughout its history, the [first-past-the-post system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_system "First-past-the-post system") has prevented the party from receiving a share of parliamentary seats that reflects their share of the vote.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006139-306)

In the 1992 general election, the Lib Dems succeeded the SDP–Liberal Alliance as the third most popular party, behind Labour and the Conservatives. Their popularity never rose to the levels attained by the Alliance, but in later years their seat count rose far above the Alliance's peak, a feat that has been credited to more intelligent targeting of vulnerable seats.[[307]](#cite_note-rennard-307) The vote percentage for the Alliance in 1987 and the Lib Dems in 2005 is similar, yet the Lib Dems won 62 seats to the Alliance's 22.[[308]](#cite_note-2005election-308) This was because in 1987, the Alliance vote was fairly evenly spread throughout the country, whereas in 2005, the Liberal Democrat vote was concentrated in particular areas, allowing them to win nearly three times as many parliamentary seats as in 1987 despite getting a slightly lower share of the overall vote.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006140–141-309)

The first-past-the-post electoral system used in UK general elections is not suited to parties whose vote is evenly divided across the country, resulting in those parties achieving a lower proportion of seats in the Commons than their proportion of the popular vote (see table and graph). The Lib Dems and their Liberal and SDP predecessors have suffered especially,[[310]](#cite_note-electoralreform-310) particularly in the 1980s when their electoral support was greatest while the disparity between the votes and the number of MPs returned to parliament was significantly large. The increase in their number of seats in 1997, 2001 and 2005 was attributed to the weakness of the Conservatives and the success of their election strategist [Chris Rennard](/wiki/Chris_Rennard "Chris Rennard").[[307]](#cite_note-rennard-307) Lib Dems state that they want 'three-party politics' in the Commons;[[311]](#cite_note-protests-311)[[312]](#cite_note-2004localelection-312) the most realistic chance of power with first past the post is for the party to be "the kingmakers" in a hung parliament.[[313]](#cite_note-coalition-313) Party leaders often set out their terms for forming a coalition in such an event—[Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") stated in 2008 that the policy for the 2010 general election was to reform elections, parties and Parliament in a "constitutional convention".[[314]](#cite_note-termsforhungparliament-314)

### Local elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Local elections")]

The party had control of 31 councils in 2008, having held 29 councils prior to the 2008 election.[[323]](#cite_note-bbc20080403-323) In the [2008 local elections](/wiki/2008_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2008 United Kingdom local elections") they gained 25% of the vote, placing them ahead of Labour and increasing their control by 34 to more than 4,200 council seats—21% of the total number of seats. In [council elections](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2011 United Kingdom local elections") held in May 2011, the Liberal Democrats suffered heavy defeats in the Midlands and North of England. They also lost heavily in the Welsh assembly and Scottish Parliament.[[117]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-117) In [local elections held in May 2012](/wiki/2012_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2012 United Kingdom local elections"), the Lib Dems lost more than 300 councillors, leaving them with fewer than 3000 for the first time in the party's history.[[123]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-123) In the [2013 local elections](/wiki/2013_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2013 United Kingdom local elections"), they lost more councillors. In the [2014 local elections](/wiki/2014_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2014 United Kingdom local elections") they lost over 300 councillors and the control of two local governments.[[324]](#cite_note-324)

In the [2016 local elections](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2016 United Kingdom local elections"), the number of Liberal Democrat councillors increased for the first time since they went into coalition in 2010. The party won 43 seats and increased its vote share by 4%. A number of former MPs who lost their seats in 2015 won council seats in 2016, including former [Manchester Withington](/wiki/Manchester_Withington "Manchester Withington") MP [John Leech](/wiki/John_Leech_(politician) "John Leech (politician)")[[325]](#cite_note-manchestereveningnews.co.uk-325) who won 53% of the vote in a traditionally safe Labour seat. Leech's win was the first gain for any party in Manchester other than Labour for the first time in six years, and provided the city's majority Labour administration with its first opposition for two years.[[325]](#cite_note-manchestereveningnews.co.uk-325) [Cheadle](/wiki/Cheadle_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cheadle (UK Parliament constituency)")'s former MP [Mark Hunter](/wiki/Mark_Hunter_(politician) "Mark Hunter (politician)") also won a seat on Stockport Council.[[326]](#cite_note-326)

In the [2021 elections](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2021 United Kingdom local elections"), the BBC reported that in England's 143 councils up for election the party won 588 seats (an increase of seven) and won seven councils (an increase of one), holding Cheltenham, Eastleigh, Mole Valley, Three Rivers, Watford and Winchester and gaining St. Albans. In the [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly"), two seats were won (an increase of one).[[327]](#cite_note-327) As of 2022, the party has 2,562 councilors.[[328]](#cite_note-328)

In some council areas, some Liberal Democrat candidates stand under the banner of 'Liberal Democrat Focus Team' instead (this being one of the party's registered descriptions with the electoral commission[[329]](#cite_note-329)), the stated aim of which being to present themselves as grassroots activists, focusing on local issues along with national issues.[[330]](#cite_note-330)

### European Parliament elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: European Parliament elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/fd/GrahamWatsonMEPHead_and_Shoulders.jpg)](/wiki/File:GrahamWatsonMEPHead_and_Shoulders.jpg)

[Graham Watson](/wiki/Graham_Watson "Graham Watson"), former leader of the [Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe](/wiki/Alliance_of_Liberals_and_Democrats_for_Europe "Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe") and Secretary General of the [International Federation of Liberal Youth](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Liberal_Youth "International Federation of Liberal Youth"), was the Liberal Democrat MEP for [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England_(European_Parliament_constituency) "South West England (European Parliament constituency)") and the first Lib Dem to be elected to the European parliament.

As a [pro-European](/wiki/Pro-European "Pro-European") party,[[331]](#cite_note-331)[[332]](#cite_note-332) the Liberal Democrats tended to fare badly at European Parliament elections.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook2010248-333) In the 2004 local elections their share of the vote was 29% (placing them second, ahead of Labour)[[312]](#cite_note-2004localelection-312) and 14.9% in the simultaneous [European Parliament elections](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2004 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") (putting them in fourth place behind the UK Independence Party).[[334]](#cite_note-2004euroelection-334) The results of the [2009 European elections](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") were similar with the party achieving a vote of 28% in the county council elections yet achieving only 13.7% in the Europeans despite the elections taking place on the same day. The 2009 elections did however see the party gain one seat from UKIP in the East Midlands region taking the number of representatives in the parliament up to 11.[[335]](#cite_note-335) In 2014 the party lost ten seats, leaving them with one MEP.[[336]](#cite_note-UK_European_election_results-336) Campaigning on a pro-Remain platform with the slogan "Bollocks to Brexit", the party achieved their best ever results in the [2019 election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2019 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), taking 19.6% of the vote and winning 16 seats.[[337]](#cite_note-337)

In the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") from 2004 to 2019, the party sat with the [Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe](/wiki/Alliance_of_Liberals_and_Democrats_for_Europe_group "Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe group") (ALDE) [political group](/wiki/Political_groups_of_the_European_Parliament "Political groups of the European Parliament"), which favoured further strengthening [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration").[[338]](#cite_note-338) The group's leader for seven and a half years was the [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England_(European_Parliament_constituency) "South West England (European Parliament constituency)") MEP [Graham Watson](/wiki/Graham_Watson "Graham Watson"), who was also the first Liberal Democrat to be elected to the European Parliament when he won the old [Somerset and North Devon](/wiki/Somerset_and_North_Devon "Somerset and North Devon") constituency in 1994.[[339]](#cite_note-339) Following the [2019 European elections](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2019 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), the Liberal Democrats joined [Renew Europe](/wiki/Renew_Europe "Renew Europe"), the successor group to the ALDE group.

### Scottish Parliament elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Scottish Parliament elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Official_portrait_of_Lord_Wallace_of_Tankerness_crop_2%2C_2019.jpg/250px-Official_portrait_of_Lord_Wallace_of_Tankerness_crop_2%2C_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_Lord_Wallace_of_Tankerness_crop_2,_2019.jpg)

[Jim Wallace](/wiki/Jim_Wallace "Jim Wallace") led the Scottish Liberal Democrats between 1992 and 2005.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Alex_Cole-Hamilton_MSP.jpg/250px-Alex_Cole-Hamilton_MSP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Cole-Hamilton_MSP.jpg)

[Alex Cole-Hamilton](/wiki/Alex_Cole-Hamilton "Alex Cole-Hamilton") has led the Scottish Liberal Democrats since 2021.

The inaugural [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") election was held in [1999](/wiki/1999_Scottish_Parliament_election "1999 Scottish Parliament election") and resulted in the Scottish Liberal Democrats winning a total of 17 seats.[[341]](#cite_note-341) The Scottish Liberal Democrats subsequently formed a [coalition government](/wiki/Dewar_government "Dewar government") with [Scottish Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour").[[342]](#cite_note-1999-2007scotland-342) Scottish Liberal Democrat leader [Jim Wallace](/wiki/Jim_Wallace "Jim Wallace") became [deputy first minister](/wiki/Deputy_First_Minister_of_Scotland "Deputy First Minister of Scotland") of the new [Scottish Executive](/wiki/Scottish_Executive "Scottish Executive"), a position he held until his resignation as party leader in 2005. Wallace served briefly as acting first minister following the death in office of [Donald Dewar](/wiki/Donald_Dewar "Donald Dewar") in 2000 and the resignation of [Henry McLeish](/wiki/Henry_McLeish "Henry McLeish") in 2001.[[343]](#cite_note-343)

The Scottish Liberal Democrats again won 17 seats in the [2003 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2003_Scottish_Parliament_election "2003 Scottish Parliament election") and again formed a [coalition government](/wiki/Second_McConnell_government "Second McConnell government") with Scottish Labour.[[344]](#cite_note-344)[[345]](#cite_note-345) [Nicol Stephen](/wiki/Nicol_Stephen "Nicol Stephen") was elected party leader in 2005.[[346]](#cite_note-nicolstephen-346) Stephen served as deputy first minister for two years. The Scottish Liberal Democrats exited government in 2007 despite losing only one seat in the [2007 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2007_Scottish_Parliament_election "2007 Scottish Parliament election"). The Scottish National Party emerged from the election as the largest party and formed a [minority administration](/wiki/First_Salmond_government "First Salmond government"). [Nicol Stephen](/wiki/Nicol_Stephen "Nicol Stephen") resigned as party leader the following year.[[347]](#cite_note-347)

[Tavish Scott](/wiki/Tavish_Scott "Tavish Scott") was elected party leader in 2008.[[348]](#cite_note-348) Scott resigned following what he described as "disastrous" results in the [2011 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election"), in which the Scottish Liberal Democrats were reduced to five seats. Scott claimed that the party had been "damaged" in Scotland by its decision to form a [coalition government](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") with the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") in 2010.[[349]](#cite_note-TavishScott-349) He further blamed the coalition government's [austerity programme](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme "United Kingdom government austerity programme").[[349]](#cite_note-TavishScott-349) [Willie Rennie](/wiki/Willie_Rennie "Willie Rennie"), who became party leader in 2011, also blamed the unpopularity of the Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition.[[350]](#cite_note-350)

The Scottish Liberal Democrats contested two Scottish Parliament elections under the leadership of Willie Rennie. The party again returned a total of five seats in the [2016 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2016_Scottish_Parliament_election "2016 Scottish Parliament election").[[351]](#cite_note-351) The Scottish Liberal Democrats recorded its worst ever result in a Scottish Parliament election by returning its lowest ever tally of four seats and achieving its lowest ever share of the vote in the [2021 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2021_Scottish_Parliament_election "2021 Scottish Parliament election").[[352]](#cite_note-352) Willie Rennie resigned as leader and was succeeded by [Alex Cole-Hamilton](/wiki/Alex_Cole-Hamilton "Alex Cole-Hamilton") in 2021.[[353]](#cite_note-353)

### Senedd elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Senedd elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Jane_Dodds%2C_Brecon_%282019%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jane_Dodds%2C_Brecon_%282019%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jane_Dodds,_Brecon_(2019)_(cropped).jpg)

[Jane Dodds](/wiki/Jane_Dodds "Jane Dodds"), leader of the Welsh Liberal Democrats since November 2017

The first elections to the newly created National Assembly for Wales (now [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd")) were in 1999; the Liberal Democrats took six seats in the inaugural Assembly; [Welsh Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") won a [plurality](/wiki/Plurality_(voting) "Plurality (voting)") of seats, but without an overall majority. In October 2000, following a series of close votes, the parties formed a [coalition](/wiki/Lib%E2%80%93Lab_pact "Lib–Lab pact"), with the Liberal Democrat leader in the assembly, [Michael German](/wiki/Mike_German,_Baron_German "Mike German, Baron German"), becoming the [Deputy First Minister](/wiki/Deputy_First_Minister_of_Wales "Deputy First Minister of Wales").[[354]](#cite_note-354) The deal lasted until the 2003 election, when Labour won enough seats to be able to govern outright.[[355]](#cite_note-2003waleselection-355)

The party had polled consistently in the first four elections to the National Assembly, returning six representatives in the first three elections and five in the [2011 election](/wiki/2011_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "2011 National Assembly for Wales election"), thereby establishing itself as the fourth party in Wales behind Labour, the Conservatives and [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru"), but fell to just one seat in 2016. Between 2008 and 2016, the leader of the [Welsh Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Welsh_Liberal_Democrats "Welsh Liberal Democrats") was [Kirsty Williams](/wiki/Kirsty_Williams "Kirsty Williams"), the Assembly Member (now [Member of the Senedd](/wiki/Member_of_the_Senedd "Member of the Senedd") (MS)) for [Brecon and Radnorshire](/wiki/Brecon_and_Radnorshire_(Senedd_Cymru_constituency) "Brecon and Radnorshire (Senedd Cymru constituency)"), the Assembly's first female party leader.[[356]](#cite_note-356)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liberal_Democrats_(UK)&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Reception")]

In 2006, Whiteley et al. noted that the Liberal Democrats were "a major force in contemporary British politics".[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006170-365) Although throughout its history, the party had been relegated to third party status, they argued that it had the capability of breaking through to become one of the country's main two parties if proportional representation (or something like it) was introduced, or if either the Conservatives or Labour were severely weakened by splitting in two.[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteleySeydBillinghurst2006174–175-366)
